course_description:
This course is an applied biological foundations course aimed at postgraduate dentistry students to provide them with the background knowledge required to understand key contemporary aspects of the management of oral and craniofacial disease.

This course provides applied biological foundations for postgraduate dentistry students, equipping them with the essential background knowledge needed to understand contemporary approaches to managing oral and craniofacial diseases. Delivered externally, it enables students to learn at their own pace through online resources and interactive workshops with experts. The four-week block format prepares students for the commencement of the in-person Doctor of Dental Medicine program in the following semester.

restrictions: Restricted to students enrolled in the DMD program.

course_aims: This course aims to introduce students to the fundamental concepts of cells and organ systems, with a focus on their relevance to oral health and dentistry. Students will explore the structure and function of key biological systems, gaining an understanding of how they impact dental practice and patient care.

learning_outcomes.description: Describe the control, integration, and coordination mechanisms required for oral health.
Understand intracellular and extracellular communication in tissue injury and disease of the oral cavity.
Understand the properties of cell membranes and the application of excitable and non-excitable cells to pharmacology in dental practice.
Demonstrate an understanding of the structure and morphology of oral and craniofacial structures, in the context of regeneration, reconstruction and rehabilitation.
Demonstrate an understanding of homeostasis and control of major physiological systems to manage chronic inflammation and wound healing in the oral cavity.
Apply the concepts of physiology to oral health or disease.
Apply the pharmacological principles of drug treatments to dental practice.

assessment_details.title: Critical Essay
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: In this task, you are required to compose a 1,000-1,200 words critical essay on how basic science is applied in everyday dental practice. This task is designed to develop your ability to integrate foundational scientific knowledge with clinical reasoning and evidence-based practice. Below are some examples to get you started, but feel free to write about a topic of your choosing: How understanding mitosis, epithelial turnover, and cellular communication informs the diagnosis and management of oral mucosal lesions. How knowledge of epithelial histology supports interpretation of clinical signs such as ulceration, white lesions, or mucosal integrity in disease. How principles of drug absorption, metabolism, and excretion influence the safe and effective use of local anaesthetics, analgesics, and antibiotics in dental practice. How understanding alveolar bone composition and remodelling guides treatment planning in periodontics, extractions, implant placement, and orthodontics. How the anatomy and function of key cranial nerves support local anaesthetic techniques, diagnosis of neuropathies, and management of sensory disturbances. How vascular knowledge guides safe procedural planning, management of bleeding, and recognition of vascular complications. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Please submit the document through ePortfolio.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
